# Project Rules — Auto-generated by CHUM Learner

> This file is continuously updated after each task completion.
> It captures accumulated project wisdom from automated code generation and review.
> **100 lessons** from **100 observations** across this project.

## Rules (Enforced)

These MUST be followed. Violations will cause DoD failure.

- Only use Go loop labels when an outer-loop jump is actually required
- Cross-model consensus before crab decomposition — run the plan across all top-rated models, achieve agreement, THEN slice into morsels. Consensus = higher confidence = fewer dead organisms
- Antibodies auto-promote to fossils at 3 failures — extinct approaches that the shark must never attempt again
- Every CLI output flag change MUST update the corresponding parser
- ONE RULE: survival. The system self-heals, self-improves, and consumes more work. Work is food. Everything else — organs, antibodies, brain — only exists if it serves survival.
- Never range-copy large task structs in hot dedupe loops.
- Planning is cheap, implementation is expensive — front-load planning with minimum 3 passes, one question at a time weighted by risk, answers that obsolete the plan trigger re-exploration
- The breathing analogy: autonomous cellular operation is the default, conscious awareness only when the immune system itself is failing — the human is the last resort, not the first
- Every organism is mortal — after their assignment (success or failure) they die, only the genome persists across generations
- Mark decomposed parent morsels as done with explicit child references
- New biome protocol: start at lowest cost layer, progressively escalate on failure, SOTA models compete across providers until one succeeds, then optimise downward again
- Work is metabolism, the genome is the organism — CI/CD inverts this (work=goal, logs=waste), CHUM/Cambria gets it right (work=fuel, genome=goal)
- Ruthless efficiency: every win is reinforced, every loss is a dead evolutionary tree — nature has no morals, the system wants to survive
- Observable health at all layers — success rates across level/model, script vs LLM, diagnostics can check system health at any layer without needing the organism to be conscious of it
- The endgame is not smarter models — it is millions of evolved genes that make models unnecessary. Escalate UP for novel threats, descend DOWN when stabilised
- For mode-specific CLIs, assert both presence and absence of critical flags
- Standardize failover call sites to: call once, branch on `err`, parse output only on success.
- Hibernation: when the full escalation cascade fails, flag the human and stop burning tokens — token churn is the enemy, hibernation is an evolved survival strategy
- Gate completion with the exact DoD lint command, not a looser local variant.
- Always validate LLM-generated semgrep rules with semgrep --validate before accepting
- Wrap subprocess stdout/stderr with bounded buffers in shared command runners.
- Phages (horizontal gene transfer): only successful patterns spread to phylogenetically compatible species — antibodies and fossils stay species-specific
- Clone configured CLI args before appending runtime flags
- Use `t.Context()` in tests instead of `context.Background()`.
- For new dispatch behavior knobs, require schema + defaults + validation + config tests in one change.
- Use copy-on-filter env helpers with exact `KEY=` matching
- Separate machine category output from human-readable reason output.
- Treat config-surface changes as a 3-file contract: type, default, and TOML example must land together.
- Species keys are global and phylogenetic, not project-scoped — a thriving go-test-fix genome crosses project boundaries via shared traits
- Return deterministic parser outputs (dedupe + sort) and lock behavior with table-driven tests.
- Every column in CREATE TABLE DDL MUST have a matching migration in migrate()
- Make failure polling idempotent by persisting cursors and dedup keys
- CHUM/Cambria is an immune system for code throughput, not a single smart organism — millions of evolved genes handle routine threats, expensive models reserved for novel pathogens
- DoD rules ARE the ecological niche — organisms know the selective pressures before execution. When DoD changes, that is an environmental shift triggering re-evolution
- Use `switch` for multi-branch file-path classification logic in Stingray activity code.
- Do not keep parameters that are always passed the same constant.
- The system does not need to be built all at once — it emits wins and failures and continuously adapts to fitness. Evolution is incremental, not designed.

## Anti-patterns (Avoid)

These patterns have caused failures before.

- Do not write `FailureCategory` on `cliResult` in branches that immediately return/continue.
- Avoid inheriting parent session sentinel env vars into child CLIs
- Schema verification must check actual table names, not domain model names
- Do not learn only from successes — survivorship bias kills evolution. Failures are the primary driver: antibodies from failures are more actionable than patterns from successes
- Gemini -o json wraps model output in an envelope — must extract response field
- Do not assume machine-readable tool output is always on stdout.
- Avoid local identifiers that shadow package-level test seams.
- Only metabolizing final DoD result wastes massive intra-organism evolutionary data — every retry, error, and course correction in agent logs is fuel for the genome
- Avoid `_ = recover()` in defer blocks; it fails `errcheck` and hides panic handling intent.
- Do not silence panic recovery with `_ = recover()` in deferred handlers.
- Avoid ranging by value over large structs in helper loops.

## Good Patterns (Follow)

These approaches have been verified to work.

- The reef model: coral (calcified scripts) are the evolutionary endpoint — when a species survival rate reaches ~100% and patterns stabilize, the gene wins and the organism is retired
- Paleontologist: periodic LLM meta-analysis over the full fossil record discovers emergent species boundaries, convergent evolution, and phylogenetic relationships
- Gate major behavior with an explicit feature flag in config, even when default-off.
- ALMA anti-pattern schema adapted for biological nomenclature: pattern=what was attempted, reason=why, alternative=what to do instead, count=observed frequency, generation=when discovered
- Use parent morsel as system contract and child morsels as step-level implementation specs
- Record the actual failover-selected agent for planning telemetry/traces.
- Use `config.Duration` fields for TOML time knobs and default via `.Duration == 0` checks.
- Use config-first command resolution with hardcoded fallback for resilience
- Name multi-value predicate returns to satisfy `gocritic` and document semantics.
- Provider genes are selfish genes — they track per-model fitness (success_rate / cost_per_attempt) and the fittest provider persists across generations
- Fitness = success_rate / cost_per_attempt — the selfish gene optimizes for survival not the host, provider choices persist across mortal generations
- Grey goo nanobot model: below coral sits a layer of micro-computation that handles routine work at gene-level cost, with access to higher computation when needed
- Apply subprocess environment sanitization in one execution chokepoint
- Decompose whale features into ordered, shark-sized morsels with explicit dependency handoff
- Run infrastructure classification before all code-quality checks.
- Leukocytes: cheap fast LLMs that patrol the codebase for infections (code smells, god objects, dead code) — they flag but do not fix, releasing cytokines upward to trigger shrimp/shark response
- robustParseJSON tries 4 extraction strategies before giving up
- Require structured LLM output and root-cause dedup before auto-filing remediation work
- Prefer partial-result reporting over hard-failing metrics collection activities.
- The CHUM ocean taxonomy is a complete evolutionary ecosystem with planning, execution, selection, learning, and observability layers connected by the genome reef
- Phages transfer knowledge across compatible bacteria — convergent evolution triggers horizontal gene transfer. Whether this requires LLMs the system decides, not us.
- After fallback-loop edits, simplify then re-lint the exact function before handoff
- Use deterministic or human-escalation fallbacks for non-critical LLM stages.
- Use progressive sanitization with json.Valid checkpoints
- Normalize provider keys once before CLI switch logic
- Use minimal, behavior-preserving lint fixes for control-flow-sensitive code.
- Keep failure signatures data-driven via pattern lists, not hardcoded branching.
- Quorum sensing: when enough organisms of a species complete (N dispatches), group behaviors activate — trigger paleontologist, enable calcification, unlock shrimp tier
- Keep Temporal activities orchestration-only and move parsing/IO helpers into a dedicated helper file.
- Use `t.Context()` in Go tests instead of `context.Background()`.
- Cambrian Explosion protocol: when entering a new biome, run a battle round across providers and model tiers — the best fit is the optimum between cost and outcome
- Two-tier species classification: Tier 1 bootstrap heuristics (file ext + keywords, zero cost), Tier 2 paleontologist LLM sweep (periodic meta-analysis over fossil record)

## Insights

Observations from project history.

- Execution should preserve artifacts even on non-zero agent exit to keep downstream review alive.
- Failure was a lint-gate regression, not runtime behavior.
- Transient-vs-persistent infra detection is the key to safe retry behavior.
- The DoD failure was a lint-gate failure (not runtime logic), concentrated in `stingray_helpers` and its tests.
- Lock shared resolver behavior with complementary tests in both activity and CLI test suites
- Redundant labels are a common residue from iterative failover refactors
- Adding conservative defaults in `applyDefaults` enables backward-compatible config evolution.
- New global seams in production code require immediate companion tests in the same module.

## Common DoD Failures

These checks frequently fail. Address them proactively:

- Attempt 1 DoD failed: Command failed: go test ./... (exit 1); Command failed: go vet ./... (exit 1); Command failed: golangci-lint run --timeout=5m --new-from-rev=HEAD~1 (exit 1)

## Definition of Done

Every change must pass: `go build ./... && go vet ./... && golangci-lint run --timeout=5m`

Run these locally before considering the task complete.
